element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Players learn to create and modify worlds with distinctive environments before introducing life
Foundation for understanding genetics; plants form base of food chain, simpler than animals
More complex than plants; players begin creating species and observing predator-prey dynamics
Players experiment with creating self-sustaining ecosystems; emergent complexity increases
Advanced feature where players observe and control evolutionary processes over time
High-level parameter manipulation; players can radically alter universe rules and observe consequences

rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: Open-ended experimentation rhythm; tension emerges from trial-and-error ecosystem design rather than time pressure or combat. Players create their own feedback loops through genetic manipulation and environmental modification. Satisfaction comes from system stability achievement.

difficulty_curve.early_game: Tutorial-guided world and basic organism creation; low complexity genetics introduction
difficulty_curve.mid_game: Players experiment with ecosystem design; moderate difficulty as they balance multiple species and environmental parameters
difficulty_curve.late_game: Advanced evolution simulation, physics modification, and complex multi-species ecosystem management; high emergent complexity from system interactions
notable_spikes: First ecosystem collapse when predator-prey ratios are misbalanced
Complexity jump when introducing multiple species simultaneously
Physics manipulation causing unexpected system-wide consequences

notable_level_patterns.description: Game structured as open-ended experiments rather than linear levels. Players create worlds, populate with modified organisms, and observe outcomes. No failure states; experiments simply produce different results based on design choices.
Players systematically modify creature genetics (traits like speed, size, reproduction rate, diet) and test viability in environments. Each modification creates new behavioral dynamics.
Implicit pattern: design self-sustaining ecosystem where predators and prey maintain balance. No explicit objective, but system feedback clearly indicates success/failure through population graphs.
